title: Share-Based Payments (ESOP)
description: Premium Analysis Guide: AS vs Ind AS vs IFRS vs US GAAP

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION A: Core Technical Comparison Table
tableData: Choice between intrinsic value method and fair value method.
Shift from historical leniency (intrinsic value often zero) to true economic cost measurement.
Primarily focused on employee stock options and shares.
Broad. Covers all share-based payments (employees and non-employees).
Broad. Covers all share-based payments (employees and non-employees).
Broad. Now aligned with IFRS for non-employees (ASC 505-50 superseded and integrated into ASC 718).
Intrinsic value or Fair value at grant date.
Mandatory fair value recognition limits accounting arbitrage.
Not specifically addressed; often derived from employee guidance or AS 10.
Fair value of goods/services received. If not reliably estimable, fair value of equity instruments granted.
Fair value of goods/services received. If not reliably estimable, fair value of equity instruments granted.
Generally mirrors employee model (fair value of equity instruments), simplified compared to past rules.
Better capture of vendor payments via equity.
Choice between straight-line amortization or accelerated amortization (tracking each tranche).
Must treat each tranche as a separate award (accelerated amortization).
Must treat each tranche as a separate award (accelerated amortization).
Choice between straight-line (if only service condition) and accelerated amortization.
Ind AS/IFRS force up-front loading of expense; US GAAP retains flexibility.
Can estimate at grant date or account for as they occur.
Must estimate forfeitures at grant date and true-up subsequently based on actuals.
Must estimate forfeitures at grant date and true-up subsequently based on actuals.
Policy election: estimate forfeitures OR account for forfeitures when they occur.
US GAAP election differs from strict IFRS/Ind AS requirement.
Service & performance conditions are "vesting". Market conditions & everything else are "non-vesting".
Similar concepts, slight differences in definitions of performance vs market conditions.
Market conditions (e.g., target share price) baked into grant date fair value; no expense reversal even if condition failed.
When options are offered to employees.
Shared understanding of terms and conditions; requires appropriate approval.
Grant date fixing is stricter globally.
Recognize minimum of original fair value PLUS any incremental value.
Recognize fair value of modified award (can be lower than original, though rare due to rules).
Re-pricing underwater options creates new expense under all modern standards.
Strict assessment based on obligation to settle in cash. Liability awards remeasured at fair value every period.
Complex rules based on ASC 480 and fixed monetary amount. Liability awards remeasured based on fair value or intrinsic value depending on entity type.
Liability classification injects massive P&L volatility.
Detailed rules. Subsidiary receiving services typically records equity-settled expense.
Subsidiary generally records expense based on fair value.
Prevents subsidiaries from hiding compensation costs when parent issues the shares.
Viewed as compensatory. Discount recorded as expense.
Viewed as compensatory. Discount recorded as expense.
Noncompensatory (no expense) if conditions met (e.g., discount <= 5%, offered to all).
US GAAP election can result in zero expense for ESPPs vs Ind AS/IFRS.
Extensive. Detailed assumptions, strike prices, WAEP, rollforwards.
Provides analysts with required data to rebuild true economic cost.

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION A: Core Technical Comparison Table
tableData: Could assume zero volatility (resulting in option value nearing intrinsic value).
Must estimate volatility (often using comparable peer group). Zero volatility forbidden.
Nonpublic entities have a practical expedient to use a "calculated value" using industry sector index volatility.
Often accounted for simply over service period.
Expense recognized only when the implicit or explicit condition is satisfied.
Expense recognized only when the event actually occurs.
Accrued over the vesting period in proportion to the share-based payment expense.
Recognized on the date of the event triggering the liability (usually exercise).
Equity classification maintained if withholding is up to statutory tax limit.
Equity classification maintained if withholding is up to maximum statutory tax limit.

Intrinsic Value Model Intrinsic value: Market price (200) - Exercise price (150) = 50 per option Total Intrinsic Value 100,000 × 50 = 50,00,000 Annual Expense 50,00,000 / 3 years = 16,66,667 / year Dr Employee compensation exp 16,66,667Cr Stock options outstanding 16,66,667
Fair Value Model Fair value using Black-Scholes: 70 per option Total Fair Value 70,00,000 Annual Expense 70,00,000 / 3 = 23,33,333 / year Notes No forfeiture estimate explicitly required.

Fair Value Requirement Mandates fair value method only. Cannot use intrinsic value method (unless rare circumstances). Volatility Cannot assume zero volatility for unlisted companies. Forfeitures Treatment Must estimate forfeitures and adjust expense (10% annual forfeiture) Dr Employee compensation exp 17,01,000Cr Stock options outstanding 17,01,000

Fair Value Requirement Fair value method required. Nonpublic ON Nonpublic Entity Election May use calculated value if volatility not practicable (70 / option) Forfeiture Policy Election Can choose to estimate OR recognize when forfeitures occur Graded Vesting Election Can choose straight-line OR accelerated Assumed Expense 23,33,333 / year

SECTION F: Balance Sheet Impact - Year 1 (ESOP)

tableData: Net operating cash unimpacted by non-cash ESOP expense
No cash flow from equity issuance until exercise
Total cash drain is identical across all frameworks
Net profitability drops due to larger non-cash expense
Margins depressed under standard fair value approach
Higher compensation expense reduces earnings power
Reported return on equity mechanically decreases
Valuation looks more expensive as EPS drops

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION I: Earnings Quality - Signals Table (ESOP)
tableData: ESOP expense increases after Ind AS adoption
Company previously used intrinsic value; now fair value required
Earnings are more truthful; prior periods understated compensation cost
Adjust historical comparables to fair value basis
Volatility assumption = zero for unlisted (under AS)
Options likely undervalued; expense too low
Recompute using peer volatility for true cost
Expense not adjusted for leavers; overstated liability
Estimate own forfeiture rate and adjust
High use of graded vesting with straight-line (under AS)
Expense recognized slower than economic vesting
Recompute using accelerated method for comparability
Possible missing expense for vendor/consultant payments
No expense recognized; IFRS peers would expense
Add back estimated ESPP cost for peer comparison

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION J: Trend Distortion Matrix - ESOP
tableData: Permanent level shift (if switching from intrinsic to fair value)
Entire vesting period of all outstanding grants
Recast historical intrinsic-based expense to fair value
Use fair-value based EPS for all periods
Fair value method creates larger equity balance

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION K: Ratio Interpretation Guide - ESOP
tableData: Recalculate using intrinsic method for old periods
Compare only to peers using same method
Recognize equity increases by same amount as expense reduces retained earnings
No real tax impact (ESOP expense deductible per tax rules on exercise)
Tax rate distortion is timing only
High ratio may indicate over-reliance on equity compensation

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION L: Analyst Concerns & Answers - ESOP
tableData: Why is ESOP expense higher under Ind AS than old GAAP?
Old GAAP allowed intrinsic value (50/option); Ind AS requires fair value (70/option)
Can companies still use zero volatility for unlisted?
No - Ind AS 102 and IFRS 2 prohibit this exception
Must use comparable listed company volatility
How do I compare Indian company under Ind AS with US company under ASC 718?
Broadly similar, but US GAAP has more policy elections (forfeiture, graded vesting, ESPP noncompensatory)
Is ESOP expense real or just accounting?
ESOP is real economic cost (dilution to existing shareholders) even though non-cash
What about group ESOPs - parent shares to subsidiary employees?
Ind AS 102 provides detailed guidance: subsidiary records equity-settled expense; parent may record cash-settled if reimbursed
Check group structure and recharge agreements
IFRS: original grant date value + incremental value; US GAAP: entire modified award value
US GAAP may produce higher expense after modification

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION M: Investor Perception Analysis - ESOP
tableData: Recognize change is accounting, not economics
Calculate fully diluted shares including in-the-money options
Use fair value expense for compensation analysis
Lower EBITDA, higher leverage ratios (if equity reduced)
More cautious if covenants based on reported numbers
Adjust EBITDA add-back for non-cash ESOP
High ESOP expense reduces reported profits
Underwrite with add-back but track dilution

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION N: Reporting Signals Decoder - ESOP
tableData: Switched from intrinsic to fair value before Ind AS adoption
Wants comparable numbers; may be hiding that old expense was too low
Recast prior periods to fair value
Uses graded vesting with straight-line amortization (under AS or US GAAP election)
Later years get less expense than economic vesting
Low volatility assumption for unlisted (under AS)
Parent grants options to subsidiary employees - no recharge
Expense may be missing from subsidiary or double-counted
No expense for plans that IFRS would expense

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION P: Red Flags vs Green Flags (Visual Indicator Matrix)
tableData: ESOP expense systematically ignored in management 'Adjusted EBITDA'
Do not ignore it; treat recurring grants as cash compensation in valuation models.
Sudden massive modification of ESOP terms (repricing)
Management bailing themselves out of poor stock performance at shareholder expense.
ESOP vesting heavily skewed to short-term or time-based rather than performance
Misalignment with long-term shareholder value creation.
Clear linkage of ESOP vesting to long-term Return on Capital (ROCE) targets
High confidence in management's alignment with long-term business health.
Dilution accurately and conservatively modeled in long-term share count projections
Use fully diluted share count for DCF per-share values.
